sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente, con l'Iran che blocca lo Stretto di Hormuz e gli Stati Uniti che rispondono con azioni militari. Il VIX è sopra 27, indicando un mercato difensivo. Il prezzo del petrolio è in spike, raggiungendo $103.14 per il Brent, il che potrebbe portare a un aumento dell'inflazione e a una recessione.
sentiment.reason_en: The market sentiment is risk-off due to increasing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East, with Iran blocking the Strait of Hormuz and the US responding with military actions. The VIX is above 27, indicating a defensive market. The oil price is spiking, reaching $103.14 for Brent, which could lead to higher inflation and recession.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato azionario è in calo, con l'S&P 500 che perde lo 0.61% e il VIX che aumenta dello 0.37%. Il prezzo del petrolio è in aumento del 2.67%, raggiungendo $103.14 per il Brent. Il dollaro si rafforza, con il DXY che guadagna lo 0.13%. I tassi di interesse sono in aumento, con il US 10Y Yield che sale dello 0.28%.
market_impact_summary.en: The stock market is down, with the S&P 500 losing 0.61% and the VIX increasing by 0.37%. The oil price is up by 2.67%, reaching $103.14 for Brent. The dollar is strengthening, with the DXY gaining 0.13%. Interest rates are rising, with the US 10Y Yield increasing by 0.28%.

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno e benvenuti in questo aggiornamento finanziario di oggi, 14 marzo 2026. Il sentiment di mercato è decisamente risk-off, con un indice di paura VIX che raggiunge quota 27.19, un chiaro segnale di un mercato difensivo. Questo cambio di sentiment è principalmente dovuto all'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche in Medio Oriente, dove l'Iran ha bloccato lo Stretto di Hormuz e gli Stati Uniti hanno risposto con azioni militari. 

  Tre dati chiave per iniziare: 
  1. Il prezzo del petrolio Brent è salito a $103.14, con un aumento del 2.67%, il che potrebbe portare a un aumento dell'inflazione e a una recessione.
  2. L'indice DXY è salito a 100.50, con un aumento dello 0.13%, segno di una maggiore domanda di dollari in questo momento di incertezza.
  3. Il mercato azionario americano, rappresentato dall'S&P 500, è sceso a 6632.19, con un calo dello 0.61%, riflettendo la preoccupazione degli investitori per la situazione geopolitica.

  Passando ai mercati, analizziamo i principali asset. 
  La coppia EUR/USD è scesa a 1.1423, con un calo dello 0.85%, mentre il Dollar Index (DXY) è salito a 100.50, con un aumento dello 0.13%. 
  L'S&P 500, indice rappresentativo del mercato azionario americano, è sceso a 6632.19, con un calo dello 0.61%. 
  Il VIX, indice di volatilità, è sceso leggermente a 27.19, con un calo dello 0.37%, ma rimane su livelli elevati. 
  I titoli di stato americani a 20 anni (TLT) sono scesi a 86.54, con un calo dello 0.49%, e il rendimento del decennale americano è salito al 4.2850%, con un aumento dello 0.28%. 
  L'oro, bene rifugio per eccellenza, è salito a $5061.70, con un aumento dello 0.18%. 
  Il Bitcoin è sceso a $70,611, con un calo dello 0.50%. 
  Il prezzo del petrolio Brent, come già menzionato, è salito a $103.14, con un aumento del 2.67%. 
  Gli indici europei, come lo STOXX 600, sono scesi a 59.13, con un calo dello 0.55%, mentre il Nikkei giapponese è sceso a 53,820, con un calo dell'1.16%. 
  Lo Shanghai Composite cinese è rimasto stabile a 4095.45. 
  I BTP italiani a 10 anni hanno registrato un rendimento del 3.7930%, con un aumento dell'1.07%.

  Dal punto di vista geopolitico, la situazione in Medio Oriente continua a essere tesa. L'Iran ha bloccato lo Stretto di Hormuz, un passaggio critico per il trasporto del petrolio, in risposta alle azioni militari degli Stati Uniti. Questo evento ha immediatamente impattato sul prezzo del petrolio, spingendolo al rialzo. 
  La visita del leader giapponese negli Stati Uniti, prevista per discutere la situazione in Medio Oriente e le tensioni con la Cina, assume particolare importanza in questo contesto.

  Sul fronte macroeconomico e delle banche centrali, la Federal Reserve americana ha mantenuto invariato il Fed Funds Rate al 3.64%. 
  I dati macroeconomici recenti negli Stati Uniti hanno mostrato un CPI YoY del 2.7%, in linea con le aspettative, e un calo dei Non-Farm Payrolls a -92K, peggiore delle previsioni. 
  Il tasso di disoccupazione è salito al 4.4%. 
  La prossima uscita importante sarà il PCE Core YoY, prevista per il 28 marzo.

  Guardando al futuro, sarà cruciale monitorare l'evoluzione della situazione geopolitica in Medio Oriente e le sue ripercussioni sui mercati finanziari. 
  Domani, gli investitori terranno d'occhio ulteriori sviluppi sulla crisi ucraina e le possibili risposte delle banche centrali. 
  La liquidità globale, misurata dal Global M2 Liquidity, continua a espandersi, raggiungendo quota $22.4T, con un aumento del 4.3% su base annua negli Stati Uniti.
audio_script_en:
Good morning, and welcome to today's edition of Bloomberg Radio. I'm your host, and we're coming to you live on March 14th, 2026.

The market sentiment is decidedly risk-off today, scoring a 7 out of 10, as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate. The Strait of Hormuz, a critical waterway for global oil shipments, has been blocked by Iran, prompting a military response from the United States. This development has sent the VIX index soaring above 27, indicating a defensive market stance. The price of oil is spiking, with Brent crude reaching $103.14 per barrel, which could potentially lead to higher inflation and even recession.

Let's take a look at the key market data. The EUR/USD exchange rate is currently at 1.1423, down 0.85% on the day. The Dollar Index, which measures the greenback's value against a basket of major currencies, is at 100.50, up 0.13%. The S&P 500 index is trading at 6632.19, down 0.61%, while the VIX index is at 27.1900, down 0.37%. The TLT Bond USA 20Y is at 86.5400, down 0.49%, and the US 10Y Yield is at 4.2850%, up 0.28%. Gold is trading at $5061.70 per ounce, up 0.18%, while Bitcoin is at $70,611, down 0.50%. The STOXX 600 index is at 59.1300, down 0.55%, and the NIKKEI is at 53,820, down 1.16%. The SHANGHAI index is at 4095.45, unchanged on the day.

In terms of macro data, the US CPI YoY is at 2.7%, down from 2.8% previously. The Core CPI YoY is also at 2.7%, down from 2.9%. The Non-Farm Payrolls have shown a decline of 92K, down from 126K previously. The Unemployment Rate is at 4.4%, up from 4.3%. The Fed Funds Rate is at 3.64%, unchanged.

Now, let's move on to the geopolitical front. The situation in the Middle East is becoming increasingly complex, with Iran blocking the Strait of Hormuz and the US responding with military actions. This development has significant implications for global oil supplies and prices. The Japanese leader is set to visit Washington, and it's likely that the Middle East crisis will be high on the agenda.

The macroeconomic implications of this crisis are far-reaching. A prolonged conflict in the Middle East could lead to a global energy shock, with significant consequences for inflation and economic growth. The Iran war could also trigger a global food crisis, as sanctions and trade disruptions impact agricultural supplies.

In Europe, the ECB is likely to be closely monitoring the situation, as the region's economy is heavily dependent on imported oil. The BTP 10Y yield is at 3.7930%, up 1.07%, indicating a rise in Italian bond yields.

The Global M2 Liquidity proxy is expanding, currently at $22.4T, with a YoY increase of 4.3% in the US. This expansion in liquidity could potentially offset some of the negative economic impacts of the Middle East crisis.

Looking ahead, it's essential to monitor the situation in the Middle East closely, as any escalation could have significant consequences for global markets. The US PCE Core YoY data is due to be released on March 28th, and this will provide valuable insights into the state of the US economy.

The GDP QoQ USA data is due to be released on April 29th, and this will provide a comprehensive picture of the US economy's performance.

In conclusion, the market sentiment is risk-off today, driven by escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. The situation is complex, and it's essential to monitor developments closely. The macroeconomic implications of this crisis are far-reaching, and it's crucial to stay informed.

That's all for today. We'll be back with more news and analysis after this break. Stay tuned.

articles.title: Cold Hard Truths: Arctic Security in a Changing World
articles.snippet: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Gabriella Gricius, Senior Fellow at the Arctic Institute, to discuss Arctic security, Donald Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, Russia’s and China’s interests in the High North, and how NATO and European capitals are responding.

articles.title: A New Global Energy Shock
articles.snippet: When the United States and Israel launched their war of choice against Iran, the Islamic Republic pursued the most effective response at its disposal: triggering a spike in global energy prices by attacking Gulf energy infrastructure and blocking the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. While the long-term effects on energy markets remain difficult to predict, rising energy prices and interest rates have provided US President Donald Trump with powerful reasons to seek an off-ramp from a war opposed by most Americans, including his MAGA base. But with Israel committed to “total victory” and a defiant Islamic Republic now mining the strait, a cascade of global economic disruption appears likely.

articles.title: Japanese Leader Wrestles with Middle East Shock in Planned Trip to Washington
articles.snippet: Crisis Group expert Matt Wheeler assesses the importance of Sanae Takaichi’s forthcoming visit to the White House, amid war, tensions with China and U.S. policy shifts

articles.title: The Iran War Could Trigger a Global Food Crisis
articles.snippet: While media coverage of Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz has focused on oil prices, the implications for global food supplies are no less alarming. A prolonged closure could disrupt agriculture worldwide and place more than 100 million people at risk of a humanitarian catastrophe.

articles.title: Sudan’s devastating war rages on as regional rivalries deepen
articles.snippet: Yet the Iran crisis may push Gulf rivals to set aside their differences and revive stalled diplomacy on Sudan.

articles.title: The Gulf’s Tough Choices
articles.snippet: For the Gulf Cooperation Council's six member states, strategic patience may be the wisest approach to the Iran war for now. But the longer the war goes on, the more the region’s credibility as a stable global hub erodes – a vulnerability that the Islamic Republic is determined to exploit.

articles.title: Military Senior Service Colleges Require Reform, But There Sure Are Some Bad Ideas Out There
articles.snippet: “No one is thinking if everyone is thinking alike.” Gen. George S. Patton’s quip shows that he not only understood the advantage of audacious combat leadership, but the necessity to develop leaders and organizations that think critically and challenge assumptions. Military education is at the forefront of this endeavor in professional militaries. Indeed, last week Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth ordered a review of the military’s senior service/war colleges and the severing of ties between the department and 22 academic institutions in an effort to better prepare strategic leaders for the complexities of modern warfare.In a recent opinion article, Thomas The post Military Senior Service Colleges Require Reform, But There Sure Are Some Bad Ideas Out There appeared first on War on the Rock

articles.title: The Spheres-of-Influence Illusion
articles.snippet: For the first time since 1945, the United States, China, and Russia are converging around an authoritarian conception of sovereignty that relies on raw power rather than international law. But history suggests that dividing the world into rival blocs is more likely to produce conflict than stability.

articles.title: Europe rebukes US for temporarily lifting sanctions on Russian oil
articles.snippet: German chancellor says decision is wrong and that pressure on Putin over Ukraine war should be increased Middle East crisis – live updates European countries have pushed back against Donald Trump’s decision to ease some US sanctions on Russian oil amid Iran’s blockade of the strait of Hormuz, insisting the international community should maintain pressure on Moscow over its war against Ukraine. The UK has joined Germany, France and Norway in rejecting the move, with the foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper, decrying what she said was Russia and Iran’s attempt to “hijack the global economy”. articles.title: Trump Bombs Iran’s Kharg Island and Threatens to Hit Oil Exports
articles.snippet: U.S. President Donald Trump said American forces had launched a major bombing raid on Iran’s strategic Kharg Island, targeting military facilities on the key Persian Gulf outpost while warning Tehran that its vital oil infrastructure could be destroyed if shipping in the Strait of Hormuz is disrupted. In a social media statement, Trump said that at his direction the United States Central Command carried out “one of the most powerful bombing raids in the history of the Middle East,” claiming U.S. forces had “totally obliterated…

articles.title: Bank of America: Sell Oil Above $100
articles.snippet: Bank of America is warning investors not to chase oil prices higher if crude climbs above $100 per barrel, arguing that prices at those levels tend to trigger government and central bank responses aimed at cooling the economy. The bank said oil has already been one of the biggest market movers this year. Crude prices are up nearly 70% year-to-date, far outpacing broader commodities, which are up about 41%, and gold, which has gained roughly 17%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 has fallen about 2.5%, while bitcoin has dropped around 20%. According…

articles.title: The World’s Biggest Oil Supply Shock Is Now Unfolding
articles.snippet: The International Energy Agency (IEA) has warned that the current energy crisis triggered by the war in the Middle East marks the “largest disruption” in the history of the oil market. The group made a record intervention in the market on Wednesday, with the release of 400m barrels from the strategic reserves. But the move has made little difference to the surging price of Brent crude – the international benchmark for oil – which once again eclipsed the $100 mark on Thursday on the news two Iraqi tankers had been struck…

articles.title: China’s Sinopec to Slash Refinery Rates amid Crude Supply Shock
articles.snippet: The top Asian refiner, China’s state-controlled Sinopec, plans to slash this month its refinery processing rates by 11-13% as crude supply is choked by the Middle East war, Reuters reported on Friday, quoting sources with knowledge of the refining giant’s operations. Sinopec, whose refineries account for a third of all Chinese throughput, plans to lower its crude runs by between 600,000 and 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) in March, from initial plans to process 5.2 million bpd. The loss in refinery throughput does not include regular…

articles.title: Iran’s All-or-Nothing Game in the Strait of Hormuz
articles.snippet: Brent crude is set to close above $100 for the first time since 2022 as tensions around the Strait of Hormuz intensify. ICE Brent is set to settle above $100 per barrel for the first time since August 2022, undeterred by the US’ easing of sanctions on Russia and the IEA’s mulled release of strategic petroleum stocks. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz sits front and centre to any oil price outlook, with Iran already striking at least 18 vessels since the US-Iran conflict started two weeks ago.
